summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

A class of 40 students is surveyed. 16 students prefer blue. What is the relative frequency of students who prefer blue?

Relative frequency = 16/40 = 0.40, or 40%.

In a two-way table with a grand total of 200, the joint frequency for 'Male & Agrees' is 50. What is the joint relative frequency?

Joint relative frequency = 50/200 = 0.25 = 25%.

A two-way table shows: A & X = 30, A & Y = 20, B & X = 25, B & Y = 25. What is the marginal relative frequency for row A?

Row A total = 30 + 20 = 50. Grand total = 30 + 20 + 25 + 25 = 100. Marginal relative frequency = 50/100 = 0.50.

Out of 80 surveyed people, 20 are male and prefer coffee. What is this joint relative frequency expressed as a percentage?

Joint relative frequency = 20/80 = 0.25 = 25%.

In a two-way table, 45 out of 60 females chose option A. What is the conditional relative frequency of choosing option A given the person is female?

Conditional relative frequency = 45/60 = 0.75, or 75% of females chose option A.

A two-way table has a grand total of 500. The marginal frequency for 'Disagree' is 150. What is the marginal relative frequency for 'Disagree'?

Marginal relative frequency = 150/500 = 0.30, or 30%.

A relative frequency table shows these joint relative frequencies: 0.15, 0.25, 0.35, and one unknown value. What is the missing value?

All joint relative frequencies must sum to 1. So the missing value = 1 - 0.15 - 0.25 - 0.35 = 0.25.

What is 'relative frequency'?

Relative frequency is the proportion of a category's count to the total number of observations. It can be expressed as a fraction, decimal, or percentage.

What is 'joint relative frequency'?

Joint relative frequency is the ratio of a specific cell's count to the grand total, representing the proportion of all observations that fall in that particular combination of categories.

What is 'marginal relative frequency'?

Marginal relative frequency is the proportion of a row total or column total relative to the grand total. It summarizes the distribution of one variable regardless of the other.

What is 'conditional relative frequency'?

Conditional relative frequency is the proportion of a cell's count relative to its row or column total, showing the frequency of one variable given a specific category of the other.

A high school surveys 250 students about favorite subject (Math or English) and grade level (Junior or Senior). Results: Junior & Math = 60, Junior & English = 50, Senior & Math = 70, Senior & English = 70. What is the joint relative frequency of Senior & Math?

Joint relative frequency of Senior & Math = 70/250 = 0.28.

A hospital surveys 400 patients about satisfaction (satisfied or dissatisfied) and department (ER or outpatient). Results: ER & Satisfied = 80, ER & Dissatisfied = 120, Outpatient & Satisfied = 150, Outpatient & Dissatisfied = 50. What is the conditional relative frequency of being satisfied given the patient visited the ER?

ER total = 80 + 120 = 200. Conditional relative frequency = 80/200 = 0.40 = 40%.

A store tracks customer purchases by product type (electronics or clothing) and customer type (new or returning). Results: New & Electronics = 45, New & Clothing = 30, Returning & Electronics = 55, Returning & Clothing = 70. What is the marginal relative frequency for returning customers?

Grand total = 45 + 30 + 55 + 70 = 200. Returning total = 55 + 70 = 125. Marginal relative frequency = 125/200 = 0.625.

A company surveys 300 employees about work preference (remote or in-office) and role (manager or non-manager). Results: Manager & Remote = 15, Manager & In-office = 35, Non-manager & Remote = 120, Non-manager & In-office = 130. What is the conditional relative frequency of preferring remote work given the employee is a manager?

Manager total = 15 + 35 = 50. Managers preferring remote = 15. Conditional relative frequency = 15/50 = 0.30 = 30%.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: Out of 80 surveyed people, 20 are male and prefer coffee. What is this joint relative frequency expressed as a percentage?

Joint relative frequency = 20/80 = 0.25 = 25%.

Example: A company surveys 300 employees about work preference (remote or in-office) and role (manager or non-manager). Results: Manager & Remote = 15, Manager & In-office = 35, Non-manager & Remote = 120, Non-manager & In-office = 130. What is the conditional relative frequency of preferring remote work given the employee is a manager?

Manager total = 15 + 35 = 50. Managers preferring remote = 15. Conditional relative frequency = 15/50 = 0.30 = 30%.

Example: A school district examines test results (pass or fail) by school type (urban or suburban). Results: Urban & Pass = 180, Urban & Fail = 120, Suburban & Pass = 250, Suburban & Fail = 50. Using conditional relative frequencies, which school type has a higher pass rate?

Urban: 180/300 = 60%. Suburban: 250/300 = 83.3%. Suburban schools have a higher pass rate.

Example: A retail chain surveys 600 customers about shopping method (online or in-store) and age group (under 40 or 40 and over). Results: Under 40 & Online = 180, Under 40 & In-store = 120, 40+ & Online = 90, 40+ & In-store = 210. What percentage of all customers shop online?

Total online = 180 + 90 = 270. Marginal relative frequency = 270/600 = 0.45 = 45%.

Example: An insurance company examines claims data for 1,000 policyholders by age (under 25 or 25+) and claim status (filed claim or no claim). Results: Under 25 & Claim = 80, Under 25 & No Claim = 120, 25+ & Claim = 200, 25+ & No Claim = 600. Which age group has a higher claim rate, and what are the rates?

Under 25: 80/200 = 40% filed claims. 25+: 200/800 = 25% filed claims. The under-25 group has a higher claim rate.

summary_sections.title: Special Topics
summary_sections.content_html:
Important Concepts

A class of 40 students is surveyed. 16 students prefer blue. What is the relative frequency of students who prefer blue

Relative frequency = 16/40 = 0.40, or 40%.

In a two-way table with a grand total of 200, the joint frequency for 'Male & Agrees' is 50. What is the joint relative frequency

Joint relative frequency = 50/200 = 0.25 = 25%.

A two-way table shows: A & X = 30, A & Y = 20, B & X = 25, B & Y = 25. What is the marginal relative frequency for row A

Row A total = 30 + 20 = 50. Grand total = 30 + 20 + 25 + 25 = 100. Marginal relative frequency = 50/100 = 0.50.

Out of 80 surveyed people, 20 are male and prefer coffee. What is this joint relative frequency expressed as a percentage

Joint relative frequency = 20/80 = 0.25 = 25%.

In a two-way table, 45 out of 60 females chose option A. What is the conditional relative frequency of choosing option A given the person is female

Conditional relative frequency = 45/60 = 0.75, or 75% of females chose option A.

A two-way table has a grand total of 500. The marginal frequency for 'Disagree' is 150. What is the marginal relative frequency for 'Disagree'

Marginal relative frequency = 150/500 = 0.30, or 30%.

If the conditional relative frequency of passing a test given tutoring is 0.85, and 60 students received tutoring, how many passed

Number who passed = 0.85 x 60 = 51 students.

A relative frequency table shows these joint relative frequencies: 0.15, 0.25, 0.35, and one unknown value. What is the missing value

All joint relative frequencies must sum to 1. So the missing value = 1 - 0.15 - 0.25 - 0.35 = 0.25.

What is 'relative frequency'

Relative frequency is the proportion of a category's count to the total number of observations. It can be expressed as a fraction, decimal, or percentage.

What is 'joint relative frequency'

Joint relative frequency is the ratio of a specific cell's count to the grand total, representing the proportion of all observations that fall in that particular combination of categories.

What is 'marginal relative frequency'

Marginal relative frequency is the proportion of a row total or column total relative to the grand total. It summarizes the distribution of one variable regardless of the other.

What is 'conditional relative frequency'

Conditional relative frequency is the proportion of a cell's count relative to its row or column total, showing the frequency of one variable given a specific category of the other.

What does 'two-way relative frequency table' mean

A two-way relative frequency table replaces raw frequency counts with proportions (relative frequencies), either as fractions, decimals, or percentages.

What does 'row conditional relative frequency' mean

Row conditional relative frequency is found by dividing a cell's count by the total for its row, showing the proportion within that specific row category.

What does 'column conditional relative frequency' mean

Column conditional relative frequency is found by dividing a cell's count by the total for its column, showing the proportion within that specific column category.

What does it mean when two conditional relative frequency distributions are nearly identical across rows

If the conditional distributions are nearly the same across different categories, it suggests the variables are independent and have no meaningful association.

A high school surveys 250 students about favorite subject (Math or English) and grade level (Junior or Senior). Results: Junior & Math = 60, Junior & English = 50, Senior & Math = 70, Senior & English = 70. What is the joint relative frequency of Senior & Math

Joint relative frequency of Senior & Math = 70/250 = 0.28.

A hospital surveys 400 patients about satisfaction (satisfied or dissatisfied) and department (ER or outpatient). Results: ER & Satisfied = 80, ER & Dissatisfied = 120, Outpatient & Satisfied = 150, Outpatient & Dissatisfied = 50. What is the conditional relative frequency of being satisfied given the patient visited the ER

ER total = 80 + 120 = 200. Conditional relative frequency = 80/200 = 0.40 = 40%.

A store tracks customer purchases by product type (electronics or clothing) and customer type (new or returning). Results: New & Electronics = 45, New & Clothing = 30, Returning & Electronics = 55, Returning & Clothing = 70. What is the marginal relative frequency for returning customers

Grand total = 45 + 30 + 55 + 70 = 200. Returning total = 55 + 70 = 125. Marginal relative frequency = 125/200 = 0.625.

A university surveys 500 students about housing (on-campus or off-campus) and year (freshman or upperclassman). Results: Freshman & On-campus = 150, Freshman & Off-campus = 50, Upperclassman & On-campus = 100, Upperclassman & Off-campus = 200. Compare the conditional relative frequency of living on-campus for freshmen versus upperclassmen.

Freshmen: 150/200 = 75% on-campus. Upperclassmen: 100/300 = 33.3% on-campus. Freshmen are much more likely to live on-campus.

A company surveys 300 employees about work preference (remote or in-office) and role (manager or non-manager). Results: Manager & Remote = 15, Manager & In-office = 35, Non-manager & Remote = 120, Non-manager & In-office = 130. What is the conditional relative frequency of preferring remote work given the employee is a manager

Manager total = 15 + 35 = 50. Managers preferring remote = 15. Conditional relative frequency = 15/50 = 0.30 = 30%.
